rows.v: A greeting used to say hi
heh-LOH
Hello! How are you? — A friendly greeting.
An interjection — it never changes form. Can start any conversation, formal or casual.
The safest all-purpose greeting at any time of day. 'Hi' is the more casual version; 'hey' is very informal.
A greeting used before noon
good MOR-ning
Good morning! Did you sleep well? — A morning greeting.
A fixed phrase. Note the silent rules: 'good' has a short 'oo' like in 'book'.
Used until about noon, then switch to 'good afternoon'. It's a greeting only — you don't say it when leaving.
A greeting from noon to evening
good af-ter-NOON
Good afternoon, everyone. — A midday greeting.
A fixed phrase. The stress falls on the last syllable: 'after-NOON'.
More formal than 'hello'. Common in business and polite settings between roughly noon and 6 p.m.
A greeting used after about 6 p.m.
good EEV-ning
Good evening, sir. — A polite evening greeting.
A fixed phrase. 'Evening' is two syllables: 'EEV-ning' (the middle 'e' is silent).
A polite greeting, not a farewell. When leaving at night, say 'good night' instead — they are not the same.
A word used when leaving
good-BYE
Goodbye! Have a nice day. — Said when parting.
An interjection. 'Bye' is the short, casual version; 'goodbye' is slightly more formal.
'Bye' or 'bye-bye' is far more common in speech. 'Goodbye' can sound a little formal or final.
A casual way to say goodbye
see you LAY-ter
See you later! — A relaxed goodbye.
A fixed phrase. Often shortened in speech to 'see ya' or 'see you'.
Friendly and common among friends. It doesn't literally promise to meet later — it's just a warm 'bye'.
A question asking about wellbeing
how are YOO
How are you? — I'm fine, thanks. — A polite question.
A fixed phrase. Often a greeting rather than a real question — a simple 'fine, thanks' is expected.
Frequently just a polite greeting, not a deep inquiry. The standard reply is short: 'I'm fine, thanks. And you?'
A reply meaning you are well
ime FINE
How are you? — I'm fine, thanks. — A typical reply.
'I'm' is the contraction of 'I am'. Contractions are normal and natural in spoken English.
The expected answer to 'how are you?'. Adding 'thanks' and 'and you?' makes it polite and keeps the chat going.
Said when meeting someone new
nice to MEET you
Nice to meet you! — Said at a first introduction.
A fixed phrase. The full form is 'It's nice to meet you', often shortened to 'nice to meet you'.
Said only the first time you meet someone. A common reply is 'nice to meet you too'.
A question asking someone's name
whats your NAME
What's your name? — I'm Sara. — Asking a name.
'What's' = 'what is'. Reply with 'I'm…' or 'My name is…'.
A friendly, standard question. 'I'm Sara' is the easy, natural answer — no need for the full 'my name is'.
A word of agreement
YESS
Yes, please. — Agreeing or accepting.
Invariable. 'Yeah' and 'yep' are casual spoken versions.
'Yes, please' (accepting) and 'no, thank you' (declining) are the polite pairings to memorize.
A word of refusal or denial
NOH
No, thank you. — Declining politely.
Invariable. To make a verb negative, English uses 'do not / don't' ('I don't speak').
English negates verbs with a helper: 'I don't know', not 'I know not'. This trips up many learners.
A polite word added to requests
PLEEZ
Coffee, please. — Making a polite request.
Invariable. Add it to the start or end of a request to sound polite.
Leaving out 'please' can sound rude in English, even if you don't mean it to. It's used very frequently.
An expression of gratitude
thank YOO
Thank you very much. — Expressing thanks.
A fixed phrase. 'Thanks' is the shorter, casual form.
Extremely common — English speakers say it often. 'Thanks a lot' and 'thank you so much' add warmth.
A reply to thanks
yore WEL-come
Thank you. — You're welcome. — Replying to thanks.
'You're' = 'you are'. Don't confuse it with 'your' (belonging to you).
A classic spelling trap: 'you're' (you are) vs 'your' (possessive). Casual replies include 'no problem' and 'sure'.
A phrase to get attention or apologize
ex-CUSE me
Excuse me, where's the exit? — Getting attention politely.
A fixed phrase. Used to start a question to a stranger, or to pass by someone.
Versatile: get someone's attention, squeeze past, or politely interrupt. Also said after a small mistake like a cough.
A word used to apologize
SOR-ree
Sorry, I'm late. — Apologizing.
An adjective used as an apology. 'I'm sorry' is the fuller form.
English speakers use 'sorry' a lot — for real apologies and tiny things. 'Excuse me' is for getting past or interrupting.
A phrase meaning you didn't follow
I dont under-STAND
Sorry, I don't understand. — Asking for help.
'Don't' = 'do not'. A key survival phrase when learning.
Pair it with 'Could you repeat that, please?' or 'Could you speak slowly?' to keep communicating.
A question about a language
do you SPEAK
Do you speak English? — Asking about language.
Uses the helper 'do' to form a question. Fill in any language: 'Do you speak Spanish?'.
English questions usually need 'do/does': 'Do you speak…?', not 'Speak you…?'. Languages are capitalized in English.
The number 1
WUN
One coffee, please. — Counting or ordering.
Spelled 'one' but pronounced 'wun' — an irregular spelling. Also means 'a single'.
English spelling and sound often disagree: 'one' sounds like 'wun'. Numbers don't change form for gender.
The number 2
TOO
Two tickets, please. — Counting.
The 'w' is silent: 'two' sounds exactly like 'to' and 'too'.
A famous trio of homophones: 'two', 'to', and 'too' all sound the same but mean different things.
The number 3
THREE
It's three o'clock. — Counting or telling time.
Begins with the 'th' sound, which many languages lack — tongue between the teeth.
The 'th' sound is one of English's hardest for learners. Practice 'three', 'think', 'thank'.
The number 10
TEN
I have ten dollars. — Counting.
A short, regular word. The building block for 'thirteen', 'twenty', etc.
Watch the tricky teens vs tens: 'thirteen' (13) vs 'thirty' (30) sound similar but differ in stress.
The number 100
HUN-dred
One hundred people. — A large round number.
Always used with a number before it: 'one hundred', 'two hundred'. Doesn't add 's' ('two hundred', not 'hundreds').
Say 'two hundred', never 'two hundreds', when counting. ('Hundreds of…' is only for a vague large amount.)
A question asking the time
what TIME is it
What time is it? — It's three. — Asking the time.
A fixed phrase using question word order. Reply 'It's…' + the time.
Answer with 'It's three o'clock' or just 'It's three'. 'O'clock' is only used for whole hours.
This present day
tu-DAY
I'm busy today. — Referring to the current day.
An adverb/noun of time. Pairs with 'yesterday' and 'tomorrow'.
The core time trio: 'yesterday' (past), 'today' (now), 'tomorrow' (future).
The day after today
tu-MOR-oh
See you tomorrow! — Referring to the next day.
Note the triple-letter spelling: t-o-m-o-r-r-o-w. The stress is on the middle: 'tu-MOR-oh'.
A commonly misspelled word — one 'm', two 'r's. 'See you tomorrow' is a natural goodbye.
At the present moment
NOW
I have to go now. — Referring to this moment.
An adverb of time. 'Right now' adds urgency or emphasis.
'Now' = at this moment; 'right now' = immediately. Don't confuse with 'know' (sounds different: 'noh').
The clear drink (H2O)
WAW-ter
A glass of water, please. — Ordering a drink.
An uncountable noun: say 'some water' or 'a glass of water', not 'a water' in careful English.
Pronunciation varies a lot by region ('WAW-ter', 'WAH-der'). As an uncountable noun, it has no plural.
A hot caffeinated drink
KAW-fee
A cup of coffee, please. — Ordering.
Usually uncountable, but in cafés people say 'two coffees' (meaning two cups).
Casually, 'two coffees' is fine (= two cups). 'A coffee' means 'a cup of coffee' in everyday speech.
A baked food staple
BRED
I'd like some bread. — Ordering food.
Uncountable: 'some bread' or 'a slice/loaf of bread', never 'a bread'.
Like 'water', 'bread' is uncountable. Count it with units: 'a slice of bread', 'a loaf of bread'.
The statement of what you owe
the BILL
Can I have the bill, please? — Asking to pay.
A countable noun. American English often says 'the check'; British English says 'the bill'.
'Bill' (UK) and 'check' (US) mean the same thing in a restaurant. 'Can I have…?' is a polite way to ask.
Any beverage
a DRINK
Would you like a drink? — Offering a beverage.
A countable noun. 'A drink' can mean any beverage, and often specifically an alcoholic one.
Context matters: 'let's get a drink' often implies alcohol, while 'a cold drink' is usually non-alcoholic.
Things people eat
FOOD
The food is delicious. — Talking about a meal.
Uncountable: 'some food', not 'a food'. A single item is 'a dish' or 'a meal'.
'Food' is uncountable, so no plural. Use 'meal' for a full eating occasion and 'dish' for one prepared item.
A polite way to request
I'd LIKE
I'd like a coffee, please. — Ordering politely.
'I'd' = 'I would'. 'I'd like…' is softer and more polite than 'I want…'.
Say 'I'd like…' to sound polite when ordering. 'I want…' is grammatically fine but can sound blunt or childish.
The list of available dishes
the MEN-yoo
Can I see the menu? — Asking to order.
A countable noun. In English, 'the menu' always means the list of food (unlike French/Italian).
No false-friend trap here: in English 'the menu' is simply the list of dishes you can order.
A toast, or casual thanks (UK)
CHEERZ
Cheers, everyone! — A toast before drinking.
An interjection. A toast everywhere; in Britain it also casually means 'thanks' or 'bye'.
In the UK, 'cheers' has three jobs: a toast, an informal 'thank you', and a casual 'goodbye'.
A question asking location
where IZ
Where is the station? — Asking for a place.
A fixed question phrase. Plural: 'Where are…?'.
The most useful travel question. 'Where's' is the natural contraction of 'where is'.
The room with a toilet
BATH-room
Where's the bathroom? — Asking for the toilet.
A countable noun. American English; British English often says 'toilet' or 'the loo'.
Polite words vary: 'bathroom'/'restroom' (US), 'toilet'/'loo' (UK). 'Where's the bathroom?' is universally understood.
The opposite of right
LEFT
Turn left at the corner. — Giving directions.
Works as a direction and a verb form. 'On the left' / 'turn left'.
Direction basics: 'turn left', 'turn right', 'go straight (ahead)'. 'Left' is also the past tense of 'leave'.
The opposite of left
RITE
It's on the right. — Giving directions.
Means the direction, but also 'correct' and 'a right' (entitlement). Context decides.
A busy word: direction ('turn right'), correctness ('that's right'), and entitlement ('a right'). It sounds like 'write'.
In this place
HEER
I'm here! — Indicating your location.
An adverb of place, opposite of 'there'. Sounds like 'hear'.
'Here' (this place) vs 'hear' (to listen) are homophones. 'Come here' = come to me; 'over here' points nearby.
In that place
THAIR
It's over there. — Pointing out a location.
Adverb of place. Also begins sentences: 'there is / there are' (showing existence).
Three homophones: 'there' (place), 'their' (belonging to them), 'they're' (they are). A top spelling confusion.
A stop for trains or buses
STAY-shun
The train station is nearby. — Finding transport.
A countable noun. The '-tion' ending sounds like 'shun'.
The '-tion' ending always sounds like 'shun' (station, information, nation) — a reliable pronunciation rule.
A question asking the price
how MUCH
How much is it? — Asking a price.
Use 'how much' for uncountable things and prices; 'how many' for countable things.
Key difference: 'how much' (water, money, time) vs 'how many' (tickets, people, apples). 'How much is it?' asks the price.
The speaker (first person)
EYE
I am a student. — Referring to yourself.
Always capitalized in English, even mid-sentence — a unique rule. Sounds like the letter 'I' / 'eye'.
English is the only major language that always capitalizes 'I'. It can never be dropped, unlike in Spanish or Italian.
The person spoken to
YOO
You are very kind. — Addressing someone.
Same word for one person or many, formal or informal — English has no 'tu/vous' distinction.
A relief for learners: English has just one 'you' for everyone — no formal/informal or singular/plural split.
Third person singular
HEE / SHEE
He is tall. She is kind. — Referring to a person.
'He' (male), 'she' (female), 'it' (things). Verbs add '-s' in this form: 'he speaks'.
The third-person '-s' is a classic error: 'she walks', not 'she walk'. 'It' is used for objects and animals.
The speaker plus others
WEE
We are friends. — Referring to your group.
First-person plural. The object form is 'us': 'They saw us'.
'We' (subject: we go) vs 'us' (object: they helped us). Don't confuse the two roles.
A person you like and trust
FREND
She's my best friend. — Talking about people.
A countable noun, same word for male or female. The 'i' is silent: 'frend'.
No gender forms in English — 'friend' works for anyone. The silent 'i' in the spelling is a common trap.
Parents, children, and relatives
FAM-i-lee
My family is big. — Talking about relatives.
Can take a singular or plural verb: 'my family is/are…'. Plural noun: 'families'.
A collective noun — both 'my family is large' and 'my family are coming' are acceptable, with a subtle difference.
A female parent
MUTH-er
My mother's name is Mary. — Talking about family.
A countable noun. The casual, affectionate words are 'mom' (US) or 'mum' (UK).
'Mother' is neutral/formal; 'mom' (US) and 'mum' (UK) are the warm everyday words. The 'th' is soft, as in 'the'.
A male parent
FAH-ther
My father works hard. — Talking about family.
A countable noun. The casual, affectionate word is 'dad'.
'Father' is neutral/formal; 'dad' is the everyday warm word. 'Parents' covers mother and father together.
An adult male / female
MAN / WUH-man
The man and the woman. — Referring to adults.
Irregular plurals: 'man' → 'men', 'woman' → 'women' (pronounced 'WIM-in').
Tricky: 'woman' (one) is 'WUH-man', but 'women' (many) is 'WIM-in'. The plural sound change surprises learners.
The most basic verb of existence/state
to BEE
I am, you are, he is. — Describing states.
Highly irregular: am, is, are (present); was, were (past). The most important English verb.
'To be' is the most irregular verb: I am, you/we/they are, he/she/it is. Master these forms first.
A verb of possession
to HAV
I have two brothers. — Showing possession.
Irregular in one spot: 'I/you/we/they have', but 'he/she/it has'. Past tense: 'had'.
Remember 'he/she has' (not 'have'). 'Have' is also a helper verb: 'I have eaten'.
A verb of movement
to GOH
I go to work every day. — Describing movement.
Irregular past tense: 'went'. 'He/she/it goes' adds '-es'.
The past tense is the unrelated word 'went', a very common irregular form. 'Going to' also expresses the future.
A verb of action; also a helper
to DOO
What do you do? — Asking about actions or jobs.
Irregular: 'he/she does', past 'did'. Also forms questions and negatives ('Do you…?', 'I don't…').
'Do' has two jobs: a real verb ('do the dishes') and a helper for questions and negatives ('Do you…?', 'I don't…').
A verb of desire
to WANT
I want a coffee. — Expressing a wish.
A regular verb: 'wanted' in the past. 'He/she wants' adds '-s'.
Polite tip: 'I'd like…' is softer than 'I want…' when asking for things. 'Want' can sound blunt.
A verb of enjoyment
to LIKE
I like this music. — Expressing enjoyment.
Regular: 'liked' in the past. Often followed by '-ing' or 'to': 'I like swimming / to swim'.
Unlike Spanish/Italian, English 'like' works normally: 'I like coffee' (I am the subject). Add '-ing' for activities.
A verb of necessity
to NEED
I need help. — Expressing necessity.
Regular: 'needed' in the past. Stronger than 'want' — it means something is necessary.
'Need' (necessity) is stronger than 'want' (desire). 'I need to go' means it's necessary, not just wanted.
A verb meaning ability or permission
KAN
Can you help me? — Asking ability or permission.
A modal verb — never adds '-s' and is followed by a base verb: 'she can swim', not 'cans'.
Modal verbs like 'can' break the rules: no '-s' ('he can', not 'he cans'), and no 'to' after ('can go', not 'can to go').
A verb of talking
to SPEAK
I speak a little English. — Talking about language.
Irregular past: 'spoke'. 'Speak' (a language) vs 'talk' (have a conversation).
Subtle difference: 'speak English' (a skill) vs 'talk to a friend' (converse). The past tense is 'spoke'.
A casual reply to thanks or apology
no PROB-lem
Thanks! — No problem. — A relaxed reply.
A fixed phrase. A casual alternative to 'you're welcome'.
Friendly and informal. Also reassures after an apology: 'Sorry I'm late.' — 'No problem.'
A phrase meaning 'certainly'
of CORSE
Can you help? — Of course! — Agreeing readily.
A fixed phrase. The 's' in 'course' sounds like a soft 's'. Means 'certainly' or 'naturally'.
An enthusiastic 'yes, certainly'. Be aware it can sound impatient if overused ('of course I know that').
A word meaning agreement or 'fine'
oh-KAY
Let's meet at two. — OK. — Agreeing.
Also spelled 'okay'. Extremely versatile: agreement, 'fine', or a question ('OK?').
Possibly the most international word of all. It signals agreement, checks understanding ('OK?'), or describes so-so quality.
A phrase meaning you want food
I'm HUN-gree
I'm hungry. Let's eat. — Expressing hunger.
'I'm' = 'I am'. English uses 'to be' for hunger ('I am hungry'), unlike many languages that use 'to have'.
Key difference: English says 'I AM hungry' (be), while Spanish/French/Italian say 'I HAVE hunger'. Same for thirst.
A phrase meaning you can't find your way
I'm LOST
Excuse me, I'm lost. — Asking for help.
'Lost' is the past participle of 'lose', used here as an adjective with 'to be'.
A vital travel phrase. Follow it with 'Can you help me?' or 'Where is…?' to get directions.
A phrase expressing approval
I LIKE it
I like it a lot. — Expressing approval.
'It' refers to a thing already mentioned. 'I like it' approves; 'I'd like it' requests.
Mind the difference: 'I like it' (I enjoy it) vs 'I'd like it' (I want it). One letter, opposite meaning.
A call for assistance
HELP
Help! / Can you help me? — Asking for aid.
Both a noun and a verb. Shouted alone, 'Help!' is an emergency call.
'Help!' alone is an urgent cry. Politely, use 'Can you help me?' or 'Could you help me, please?'.
A question for learning words
how do you SAY
How do you say this in English? — Learning vocabulary.
A fixed phrase — the learner's best friend for building vocabulary.
The single most useful phrase for a language learner. Use it constantly: 'How do you say ___ in English?'
